MINI MINUTES: AMIR GUPTA AND PRAVANA CAPITAL PARTNERS, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO REQUESTS FOR ADMISSION, SET NO. ONE; AND REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS

Appearances via Zoom:
Gary Berticevich of Van De Poel & Levy LLP (925.934.6102, gberticevich@vanlevylaw.com) for Amir Gupta and Pravana Capital Partners, Inc.;
Kevin Rooney of Steyer Lowenthan Boodrookas Alvarez & Smith LLP (415.421.3400, krooney@steyerlaw.com) for Leah Culver, individually and as trustee

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Amir Gupta and Pravana Capital Partners Inc.'s ("Defendants") motion to compel further responses to their requests for admission, set one, and for sanctions, is granted in part.

Plaintiff Leah Culver objected to all of Defendants' RFAs as "hopelessly ambiguous." Discovery is broad but RFAs have a special function among discovery tools; their primary purpose is to remove issues from contest at trial. Culver is correct that they demand more precision than other discovery mechanisms to be useful, but her demand for perfect clarity as to each RFA greatly overstates what is required to propound answerable RFAs.

For the following RFAs, Culver's ambiguity objection has merit, and no further response is ordered: Nos. 1 ("had access to" is ambiguous); 8 (Defendants do not define structural drawings); 12 (that the scope of the project "increased" is too imprecise); 18 ("overseeing" is too imprecise); 19-20 ("coordinate" is too imprecise).

However, Culver's ambiguity objection lacks merit as to many of the interrogatories. Culver is ordered to provide further responses to RFA Nos. 2, 3 4, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26, 27. For those RFAs where Culver's answer depends on her construing a term in a particular way (e.g. construing "YOUR Dropbox" as the Dropbox folder maintained by Culver relating to her renovation project), she may provide a response that defines the term as she understands it.

Culver shall provide the further responses ordered above within three weeks. Defendants seek sanctions. (See Code Civ. Proc., 2033.290, subd. (d).) Culver's objection to all of the responses, including many that are readily answerable and proper, lacked substantial justification. Defendants' expended fees are reasonable and the court awards them in full. Culver shall pay $1960 to Defendants within 30 days.

Proposed order submitted for review. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)

Amir Gupta And Pravana Capital Partners, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Response To Requests For Production, Set No. Two; For Production Of Documents, And Request For Sanctions
Off Calendar Apr-29-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: AMIR GUPTA AND PRAVANA CAPITAL PARTNERS, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION, SET NO. TWO; FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS; AND REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS

Appearances via Zoom:
Gary Berticevich of Van De Poel & Levy LLP (925.934.6102, gberticevich@vanlevylaw.com) for Amir Gupta and Pravana Capital Partners, Inc.;
Kevin Rooney of Steyer Lowenthan Boodrookas Alvarez & Smith LLP (415.421.3400, krooney@steyerlaw.com) for Leah Culver, individually and as trustee

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Amir Gupta and Pravana Capital Partners Inc.'s ("Defendants") motion to compel further responses to their requests for production, set two, and for sanctions, is granted.

All of the RFPs at issue appear proper at first look. In her opposition papers, Culver defends her objection-only responses on a few grounds. First, she contends that Defendants' queries about her Dropbox folder are ambiguous because they insufficiently define that term. As with Defendants' use of the term in the RFAs, the court concludes that it is not so ambiguous that the RFPs cannot be answered; Culver discussed the folder in her deposition testimony and it was apparently a term that the parties had sufficient common understanding of during the project that they were able to use the Dropbox folder to provide documents to each other, according to Culver's testimony.

Second, she contends that she has already produced responsive documents within the Dropbox folder. Her verified responses do not make this assertion. Moreover, the requests concerning uploads and downloads in the Dropbox folder do not merely seek documents in the folder; they also seek ESI concerning when those uploads occurred and who had access to them. It is not clear from Culver's opposition that she has produced this ESI.

Third, for RFPs 63-66, Culver contends that she has already produced all documents concerning communications concerning the project and documents relating to inspection of the project. Again, this is a contention made in papers signed by her counsel and does not come in a verified response. Moreover, Culver does not identify by bates number which documents are responsive to which RFP. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 2031.280, subd. (a).)

Culver is ordered to make further verified responses to all of the RFPs consistent with this order within three weeks of the order. Culver shall produce all responsive documents within 45 days to the extent not yet produced.

Defendants seek sanctions. "[T]he court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 2031.300, subd. (c).) The court does not find substantial justification and orders Culver to pay $1247.50 to Defendants within 30 days of this order.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories - Construction Litigation, Set One; Numbers 305.13, 309.2, 314.4 And 326.1; And Request For Sanctions
Off Calendar Apr-29-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: AMIR GUPTA AND PRAVANA CAPITAL PARTNERS, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO FORM INTERROGATORIES - CONSTRUCTION LITIGATION, SET ONE; NUMBERS 305.13, 309.2, 314.4, and 326.1; AND REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS

Appearances via Zoom:
Gary Berticevich of Van De Poel & Levy LLP (925.934.6102, gberticevich@vanlevylaw.com) for Amir Gupta and Pravana Capital Partners, Inc.;
Kevin Rooney of Steyer Lowenthan Boodrookas Alvarez & Smith LLP (415.421.3400, krooney@steyerlaw.com) for Leah Culver, individually and as trustee

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Amir Gupta and Pravana Capital Partners Inc.'s ("Defendants") motion to compel further responses to Construction Litigation Form Interrogatories, Set One, Nos. 305.13, 309.2, 314.4, and 326.1, and for sanctions, is granted in part.

As to No. 305.13, the court orders a further response. This interrogatory requires plaintiff Leah Culver to identify, inter alia, people she hired to repair a condition that she contends is a construction claim or construction defect claim. Culver responded by identifying the general contractor only but did not identify other people who worked on the project. In deposition, she identified other people who remodeled the home after Defendants' work ended, and made reference to additional unidentified people. (Ex. D to Berticevich Decl., p. 42.) Culver must identify people she hired to repair the condition, not merely the general contractor who supervised the work.

As to No. 309.2, this interrogatory seeks "documents [that] support the existence or amount of any item of damages claimed in" response to a previous interrogatory. Culver generally referred Defendants to all documents produced in the litigation. This can be an appropriate response, but a party invoking Code of Civil Procedure, 2030.230 by referring to writings in lieu of setting out a response must identify more particularly the documents from which the answer can be derived. A general reference to all documents produced is not sufficient. Culver must amend to identify by bates number which produced documents support the existence or amount of damages.

As to 314.4, Culver has provided a complete response. That Defendants disagree with the response or offer a competing interpretation of the termination of the parties' initial contract is not a basis for the court to require a further response.

As to 326.1, because the court orders further responses to some requests for admissions in a companion order today, Culver is ordered to provide further responses to this interrogatory.

Further verified responses, where ordered above, shall be served within three weeks.

Defendants seek sanctions of $1247.50. "The court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a further response to interrogatories, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The court finds that Culver's opposition lacked substantial justification in part and orders Culver to pay $700 to Defendants within 30 days of this order.

Proposed order submitted for review. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)

Off Calendar Mar-18-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT LEAH CULVER'S, IN HER INDIVIDUAL CAPACITY AND IN HER CAPACITY AS TRUSTEE OF THE LEAH CULVER REVOCABLE TRUST, Motion To Compel Further Responses From Pravana Capital Partners, Inc. To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One, And For An Award Of Sanctions.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Gary Berticevich, Esq., of Van De Poel & Levy LLP, (925) 934-6102, for Plaintiff/Defendant/Cross-Complainant.

Kevin F. Rooney, Esq., of Steyer Lowenthal Boodrookas Alvarez & Smith LLP, (415) 421-3400, for Leah Culver, Defendant/Plaintiff/Cross-Defendant.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Per the email dated March 12, 2026 from Kevin Rooney to the court, the only remaining issue on this motion is "whether Ms. Culver is entitled to receive bank records from Mr. Gupta and his entity (Pravana) involving other construction projects. The court resolves that issue as follows: No. Plaintiff Leah Culver has not shown that bank records unrelated to her construction project are directly relevant to any of the claims or defenses in this case. As financial records that implicate privacy concerns, bank records are typically discoverable only when they are directly relevant to a claim or defense rather than per the usual less stringent discovery standard. There is no reason to depart from the directly relevant standard for bank records in this case.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed form of order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/HEK)
